questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: what does `typeof null` tell you in JavaScript?

answers.text: The way I understand it: it returns `"object"` because of a historical compatibility quirk.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It returns `"object"` because of a historical compatibility quirk.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It returns `"object"` because of a historical compatibility quirk.

answers.text: Short version: The operator returns `"object"`, even though null is a primitive value.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The operator returns `"object"`, even though null is a primitive value.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The operator returns `"object"`, even though null is a primitive value.

answers.text: I'd go with this: The result does not mean that null behaves like a normal object.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The result does not mean that null behaves like a normal object.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The result does not mean that null behaves like a normal object.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does `const` prevent when it holds an object?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: It makes every property of the object immutable.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Object properties can still change unless the object is frozen or otherwise protected.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Object properties can still change unless the object is frozen or otherwise protected.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: The object can still be mutated through the const binding.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The object can still be mutated through the const binding.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The object can still be mutated through the const binding.

answers.text: From what I've seen, it prevents assigning a different value to that variable.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It prevents assigning a different value to that variable.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It prevents assigning a different value to that variable.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: how does `Array.prototype.sort()` compare values when no comparator is supplied?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: It returns a new sorted array and leaves the original unchanged.

feedback.legit: Not this one. The sort method rearranges the original array in place and returns that same array reference.
feedback.sus: Good catch. The sort method rearranges the original array in place and returns that same array reference.

answers.text: The way I understand it: it converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code unit sequences.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code unit sequences.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It converts elements to strings and compares their UTF-16 code unit sequences.

answers.text: Short version: It always sorts numbers from smallest to largest.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Numeric order requires a comparator such as `(a, b) => a - b`.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Numeric order requires a comparator such as `(a, b) => a - b`.

questions.prompt: Someone dropped this in the group chat: what does a JavaScript closure retain?

answers.text: The way I understand it: it retains access to bindings from its surrounding lexical environment.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It retains access to bindings from its surrounding lexical environment.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It retains access to bindings from its surrounding lexical environment.

answers.text: Short version: Closures capture bindings, so they can observe later updates to those bindings.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Closures capture bindings, so they can observe later updates to those bindings.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Closures capture bindings, so they can observe later updates to those bindings.

answers.text: I'd go with this: A closure can outlive the function call that created its environment.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A closure can outlive the function call that created its environment.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A closure can outlive the function call that created its environment.

questions.prompt: No searching: when do resolved Promise callbacks normally run?

answers.text: This is what trips people up: They run synchronously inside the call to `then`.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Promise reactions are queued rather than executed synchronously.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Promise reactions are queued rather than executed synchronously.

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: They normally run before the next timer task such as `setTimeout(..., 0)`.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They normally run before the next timer task such as `setTimeout(..., 0)`.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They normally run before the next timer task such as `setTimeout(..., 0)`.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: They run as microtasks after the current call stack finishes.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. They run as microtasks after the current call stack finishes.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. They run as microtasks after the current call stack finishes.

questions.prompt: Before I confidently say the wrong thing: how does `this` work inside an arrow function?

answers.text: I'd phrase it like this: Calling an arrow function with `call` or `apply` changes its `this` value.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Arrow functions use lexical `this`; `call`, `apply`, and `bind` cannot replace it.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Arrow functions use lexical `this`; `call`, `apply`, and `bind` cannot replace it.

answers.text: The way I understand it: an arrow function uses `this` from its surrounding lexical scope.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An arrow function uses `this` from its surrounding lexical scope.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An arrow function uses `this` from its surrounding lexical scope.

answers.text: Short version: Calling it with `new` creates a fresh `this` value.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Arrow functions are not constructors and cannot be called with `new`.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Arrow functions are not constructors and cannot be called with `new`.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what happens when one input to `Promise.all` rejects?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: The returned promise rejects with that rejection reason.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The returned promise rejects with that rejection reason.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The returned promise rejects with that rejection reason.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: The other asynchronous operations are automatically cancelled.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Promise.all does not cancel work that has already started.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Promise.all does not cancel work that has already started.

answers.text: From what I've seen, other inputs may continue running even though the aggregate has rejected.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. Other inputs may continue running even though the aggregate has rejected.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. Other inputs may continue running even though the aggregate has rejected.

questions.prompt: A teammate asked me this: what does `array.map(async item => ...)` return?

answers.text: This was the explanation that clicked for me: It waits for every callback and returns resolved values.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Use `await Promise.all(...)` when all mapped promises must resolve.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Use `await Promise.all(...)` when all mapped promises must resolve.

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: The `map` method itself remains synchronous.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. The `map` method itself remains synchronous.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. The `map` method itself remains synchronous.

answers.text: From what I've seen, it returns an array of promises.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. It returns an array of promises.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. It returns an array of promises.

questions.prompt: Forum check: are imported ES module bindings live?

answers.text: From what I've seen, the importing module may assign a new value directly to an imported binding.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Imported bindings are live but read-only from the importing module.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Imported bindings are live but read-only from the importing module.

answers.text: The practical answer is: an importer observes updates made to an exported binding by its module.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. An importer observes updates made to an exported binding by its module.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. An importer observes updates made to an exported binding by its module.

answers.text: My answer: An importer receives an unrelated copy that never changes.

feedback.legit: Not this one. ES module imports are live read-only views of exported bindings.
feedback.sus: Good catch. ES module imports are live read-only views of exported bindings.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: why can WeakMap keys help avoid retaining objects?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: WeakMap does not keep its object keys alive by itself.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. WeakMap does not keep its object keys alive by itself.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. WeakMap does not keep its object keys alive by itself.

answers.text: From what I've seen, WeakMap accepts strings as weakly held keys.

feedback.legit: Not this one. WeakMap keys must be objects or non-registered symbols, not ordinary strings.
feedback.sus: Good catch. WeakMap keys must be objects or non-registered symbols, not ordinary strings.

answers.text: The practical answer is: WeakMap keys cannot be enumerated because their lifetime is not observable that way.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. WeakMap keys cannot be enumerated because their lifetime is not observable that way.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. WeakMap keys cannot be enumerated because their lifetime is not observable that way.

questions.prompt: Let's clear this one up: can every JavaScript Proxy trap return arbitrary results?

answers.text: Short version: Violating a Proxy invariant can cause a TypeError.

feedback.sus: This one is legit. Violating a Proxy invariant can cause a TypeError.

answers.text: I'd go with this: A trap cannot report some impossible states for a non-extensible target.

feedback.legit: Yep, this one checks out. A trap cannot report some impossible states for a non-extensible target.
feedback.sus: This one is legit. A trap cannot report some impossible states for a non-extensible target.

answers.text: I think the important part is this: yes. Proxy traps completely replace the language's object rules.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Proxy traps must respect invariants, including rules for non-configurable properties.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Proxy traps must respect invariants, including rules for non-configurable properties.

questions.prompt: Settle a debate for me: when are JavaScript Atomics operations relevant?

answers.text: Pretty sure this is the key: Atomics operations work on any ordinary JavaScript array.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Atomics require a supported integer typed array backed by SharedArrayBuffer.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Atomics require a supported integer typed array backed by SharedArrayBuffer.

answers.text: From what I've seen, Atomics operations guarantee that concurrent code never blocks.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Atomics provide synchronization primitives, and operations such as Atomics.wait can block an eligible agent.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Atomics provide synchronization primitives, and operations such as Atomics.wait can block an eligible agent.

answers.text: The practical answer is: they make ordinary object property updates thread-safe.

feedback.legit: Not this one. Atomics only operate on supported typed arrays over shared memory.
feedback.sus: Good catch. Atomics only operate on supported typed arrays over shared memory.
